coredata.dc:title: Construction of carbon dots modified Cl-doped Bi2WO6 hollow microspheres for boosting photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline under visible light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Wang, Yao
Yi, Chunhui
Xu, Jinxia
Wang, Bowei
Ma, Ran
Xu, Ming
coredata.dc:description: The composition of composite photocatalysts with both broad spectral response and efficacious separation of photoinduced carriers were essential. The CDs were installed on the surface of the three-dimensional hollow microsphere nanophotocatalyst Bi2WO6 by the hydrothermal approach. Crystal structure, morphology and composition revealed the synthesis of the composite catalyst. The higher photocatalytic capability of 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 (0.0317 min−1) was observed in the degradation procedure, explained that the doping of Cl and the presence of CDs remarkably strengthened the visible light capture and depressed the rate of electron-hole pair complexation. Notably, scavenger-quenching assays results verified that activities of h+, •OH and •O2‾ were the primary actors in the degradation procedure. Ultimately, the degradation mechanism of a feasible TCH was submitted. This study presented a novel concept for the exploitation of composite nanophotocatalysts for the effective degradation of organic pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photocatalysis
Electron-hole pairs
Degradation
Tetracycline hydrochloride

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Bi2WO6, Cl–Bi2WO6 and CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 composites
Photovoltaic and photo (electric) chemical property analysis

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51678409 ), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology of China ( 19JCYBJC19800 , and 15ZCZDSF00880 ), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes ( Z1-201507 ), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin ( TD13-5042 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The XRD profiles of each BWO crystals. (b) Magnified graphic of Bi2WO6, Cl–Bi2WO6 and 5% CDs/Cl– Bi2WO6 on the (131) plane. (c) FT-IR spectra and (d) Raman image of Bi2WO6, Cl–Bi2WO6 and 0.5%CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6.

(a) TEM images of CDs. (b) (c) Images of 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 fired in the kettle for 6 h and 12 h, respectively. (d) TEM image of catalyst 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6. (e) and (f) are HRTEM images of the 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6.
Highest resolution XPS spectrum of (a) Bi 4f. (b) W 4f. (c) O 1s and (d) C 1s with Bi2WO6, Cl–Bi2WO6 and 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6.

(a) UV–Vis DR Spectrum of various Bi2WO6 crystals, inset: white sample represents pristine Cl–Bi2WO6, tan represents 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6. (b) Diagram of (αhυ)2 versus photon energy (hυ) for Bi2WO6, Cl–Bi2WO6 and 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6.
(a) PL spectra. (b) Transient photocurrent response profile. and (c) Electrochemical impedance spectra for various BWO.
(a) Diagram of various BWO crystals degrading TC. (b) TCH degradation in reactive species capture experiments.
(a) Five cycles of photodegradation of TCH by the 0.5%CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6. (b) XRD spectra of 0.5%CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 before and after four replicate degradation experiments with visible light irradiation.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6.
Possible photocatalytic mechanism scheme for catalyst 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 under visible light irradiation.
A table listing Bi-based photocatalysts and their comparative photocatalytic performance.

head.ce:title: Construction of carbon dots modified Cl-doped Bi2WO6 hollow microspheres for boosting photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline under visible light irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes/National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tiangong University, Tianjin, 300387, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin, 300350, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin, 300350, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The composition of composite photocatalysts with both broad spectral response and efficacious separation of photoinduced carriers were essential. The CDs were installed on the surface of the three-dimensional hollow microsphere nanophotocatalyst Bi2WO6 by the hydrothermal approach. Crystal structure, morphology and composition revealed the synthesis of the composite catalyst. The higher photocatalytic capability of 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 (0.0317 min−1) was observed in the degradation procedure, explained that the doping of Cl and the presence of CDs remarkably strengthened the visible light capture and depressed the rate of electron-hole pair complexation. Notably, scavenger-quenching assays results verified that activities of h+, •OH and •O2‾ were the primary actors in the degradation procedure. Ultimately, the degradation mechanism of a feasible TCH was submitted. This study presented a novel concept for the exploitation of composite nanophotocatalysts for the effective degradation of organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics (e.g., tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH)) plays various critical actions in disease prevention and growth promotion, TCH levels are associated with ecological issues, including soil contamination, water pollution even human health [1,2]. Therefore, the effective depletion of TCH in the environment is imperative for realizing the construction of ecological civilization. Currently, degradation of TCH is primarily carried out by biodegradation, physical adsorption and chemical oxidation methods [3]. Among these methods, semiconductor photocatalysis has been recognized as a promising tool for TCH elimination [4–6]. Numerous classical semiconductor photocatalysts (e.g., TiO2, g-C3N4, CuS, BiOCl, and FeWO4 etc.) were employed for the removal of target contaminants upon illumination [7]. Nevertheless, the weaknesses in the broad forbidden bandwidth, rapidly complexing rate of photogenerated carriers and higher cost of these catalysts tended to prevent their widespread utilization [8,9]. Therefore, it is desirable to develop efficient and commercially viable photocatalysts to eliminate TCH would be an optimal technique to solve these problems.

Bismuth-based compounds generated immense attraction of numerous scholars and investigators through their moderate band gap and noteworthy photocatalytic properties [10–12]. Among the diverse Bi-based photocatalysts, one of the simplest Aurivillius compounds, Bi2WO6 has captured interest for its narrowness band gap, powerful oxidation capability and exceptional crystal structure [13,14]. Despite the safety and stability revealed by Bi2WO6, it is not surprisingly effective when served as a photocatalyst alone. Therefore, several studies (e.g., F-doped BiOCl [15], F–Bi2MoO6 [16], and IX-Bi2WO6 [17], etc.) employed elemental doping to boost photocatalyst absorption of visible light as well as to dampen photogenerated carrier complexes to accomplish the goal of empowered photocatalysis [18].

Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging carbon-based nanomaterial with the remarkable reservoir and photoinduced electron transfer performances [19–23]. Due to these outstanding properties, the CDs are commonly treated as candidates for combining with traditional photocatalysts to prevent the complexation of electron-hole pairs thereby attaining the enhancement of catalytic behavior. Moreover, due to the superior photoluminescence (PL) features of CDs, catalysts complicated with them can enlarge the light absorption range [24–26]. So far, plenty of work has been published on CDs-based photocatalysts, such as BiOCl/CQDs/rGO [27], N-CDs/CdS [28], CQDs/UYTMs [29] and CQDs/Bi3O4Br [30]. Among these studies, CDs were rarely associated with revised Bi2WO6 to investigate the impact on the photocatalytic efficiency.

Series of hollow microsphere nanophotocatalysts BWO were fabricated by a gentle hydrothermal procedure in this investigation. The structure, morphology and photochemical attributes of the attained compounds were described and evaluated in detail, while TCH in wastewater treated as a model degradant for studying the catalytic activation of the photocatalysts. Besides, Bi2WO6 hollow microspheres were compared with Cl–Bi2WO6 and CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 to characterize the contribution of CDs and Cl elements in Bi2WO6 composites. Eventually, the mechanism of the photocatalytic procedure of 0.5% CDs/Bi2WO6 material was raised.

The agents employed in this work were all analytically pure and were not further purified. Sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O), Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), sodium borate (Na2B4O7), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP K-30) and polyethyleneimine were acquired from Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. Tetracycline (TCH), anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.7%), EDTA-2Na, tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), Nafion solution (5%), 1, 4-benzoquinone (BQ), anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) and isopropanol (C3H8O) were collected from McLean Biochemical Co. The potassium chloride (KCl) was acquired from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). All solutions used in the experiment were formulated with ionized water.

CDs were constructed by hydrothermal approach. In short, sodium borate (1 mmol) and polyethyleneimine (1 mmol) were dissolved in 25 mL of sonicated and ethanol to complete solubility. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a stainless steel Teflon lined autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 5 h. The reaction kettle was cooled to indoor temperature, and the liquid in the kettle was centrifuged and subsequently kept in a dialysis bag for 48 h to strip unreacted small molecules. Then freeze-dried to arrive at a brownish-yellow mucoadhesive liquid (The optimized excitation and emission of CDs was proposed by Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Bi2WO6, Cl–Bi2WO6 and CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Each hollow microsphere BWO were also availed by hydrothermal process. Technically, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (2 mmol) was disintegrated in ethylene glycol (EG, 15 mL) with vigorous agitation at indoor temperature to derive solution 1. Solution 2 was gained by vigorously stirring a mixture of ethanol (10 mL) and ethylene glycol (5 mL) combined with Na2WO4·2H2O (1 mmol), PVP (100 mg) and KCl (0.1 mmol). Further, solution 1 was poured into solution 2 and mixed, whereas 0.02, 0.08, 0.12 and 0.15 g of CDs powder were injected into the above mixture (four parallel experiments were performed simultaneously) and sonicated until fully dissipated. Ultimately, the scattered solution was shifted to a stainless steel autoclave and retained at 160 °C for 12 h. When the prescribed time was arrived, the precipitate was cooled to indoor temperature, gathered and rinsed twice in deionized water and once in anhydrous ethanol, respectively. The assembled products desiccated at 80 °C for 12 h and denoted as 0.1% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, 0.3% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, and 1% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 (this is the mass ratio of CDs to Cl–Bi2WO6), respectively. Comparatively, the nanosheets Bi2WO6 and Cl–Bi2WO6 were formulated as shown above, except for the selective addition of CDs and KCl. Then Bi2WO6 and Cl–Bi2WO6 were calcined at 450 °C for 3 h to acquire pure hollow microspheres.

The morphology of BWO were obtained by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Regulus 8100), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-1200EX, Japan) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) (by 120 kV Hitachi H7650, Japan). Raman spectra was measured on a LabRAM Aramis. The crystal structure and phase data of the samples were collected using an X-ray diffractometer (Empyrean, PANalytical B. V.). The catalyst valence was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on an ESCALab220i-XL photoelectron spectrometer. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was performed on a spectrophotometer (UH4150, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was acquired by a Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra was measured by an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Gangdong scientific and technological development).

The photocatalytic ability of the catalysts was evaluated by degrading TCH under visible light (420 nm < λ < 780 nm). Normally, 15 mg of the preparation was poured into a custom-made quartz reactor containing 50 mL of aqueous TCH solution (20 mg/L), and the resulting suspension was then stirred under the dark for 30 min to accomplish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the target contaminant solution and the catalyst. The xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300-T3, Beijing Zhongjiao Jinyuan Co., Ltd.) with 420 nm blocking filter was illuminated as the illuminator. In 10 min intervals, gathered the suspension into 6–7 mL and centrifuged to acquire the upper supernatant. Afterward, an absorbance of the supernatant was recorded at a maximum absorption wavelength of 336.5 nm utilizing a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The concealed reaction experiments as well as catalyst dosage experiments were also executed during this phase to evaluate the optimized time to obtain the adsorption-desorption equilibrium and the appropriate amount of catalyst for the degradation procedure. During the photocatalytic reaction, disodium EDTA-2Na, isopropyl alcohol, p-benzoquinone (BQ) and KBrO3 (the mass of the scavenger added was 5% of the catalyst quality) were measured for the production of vacancies (h+), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide radicals (•O2 −) and electrons (e−) from the activator.

The electrochemical section was obtained from an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua Instruments) with a three-electrode system (FTO glass with target coating (working electrode), Ag/AgCl (reference electrode) and platinum wire (counter electrode)). Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), transient photocurrent response spectra and Mott-Schottky curves were captured in 0.5 mol L-1 Na2SO4 solution, while transient photocurrent response spectra were measured under xenon lamp irradiation.

The capability of the photocatalyst was evaluated through 4 cycles to identify the stability and reusability of the photocatalyst. The photocatalysts were washed and dried with water and ethanol after each photocatalytic reaction, and then redispersion into a new 50 mL of TCH solution was carried out to measure the degradation efficiency.

The crystal structures and the purity of the phases of various BWO were illustrated by XRD profiles. The diffractograms of the synthetics were inspected (Fig. 1 (a)). The featured reflections positioned at 28.2°, 32.7°, 47.2°, 55.7° and 58.4° were confirmed corresponding to the (131), (002), (202), (133) and (262) planes, respectively. The diffraction peaks of each BWO were in accordance with the standard pattern (JCPDS No. 39-0256), which demonstrated that the primary crystalline phase remains Bi2WO6 after chlorine doping. No diffraction peak at 2θ value 26° was detected for the catalyst equipped with CDs, which was assigned to the presence of low quantity of CDs in the composite. XRD images of 2θ between 27 and 30° were enlarged so as to further verification of the impact of chlorine incorporation on Bi2WO6 crystal structure (Fig. 1(b)). The increased diffraction intensity in the (131) plane of the sample Cl–Bi2WO6 in comparison with Bi2WO6 suggested a better crystallinity. The diffraction peaks of both Cl–Bi2WO6 and 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 were shifted toward larger 2θ values, which reflected the constriction of the lattice by Bragg's law (d(h k l) = nλ/(2 sinθ)) [16]. This was likely caused by the supersession of O2− in the Bi2WO6 lattice by Cl−. Hereby, the enhanced intensity of (131) diffraction peaks as well as the shifted peak positions were indications for the effective doping of the element Cl in the Bi2WO6 structure.

The molecular structure of the complex catalysts and the successful incorporation of CDs were further clarified using FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy. In Fig. 1(c), the peaks at ∼3428 and ∼1639 cm−1 were dominated by the contribution of the stretching and bending oscillations of the O–H of the pure phase Bi2WO6, respectively. The Bi–O stretching oscillations, the bridging stretching oscillations of W–O–W and the stretching oscillations of W–O were observed at ∼440, ∼564 and ∼734 cm−1, respectively. The stretching oscillation of the C–H bond of CDs in 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 was matched to the absorption of ∼2800 cm−1 peak. For the 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, it was comprised of CDs and Cl–Bi2WO6 in the FT-IR measurements as shown. Moreover, Raman spectrum (Fig. 1(d)) was undertaken to further investigate the structure of the composition. The two strong peaks positioned at 1625 cm−1 and 1421 cm−1 classified to the G-band and D-band of carbon, which was corresponded to sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon. Through the above characterization analysis, the following conclusion may be reached: 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 were successfully synthesized.

TEM, SEM and HRTEM images were collected with the purpose of exploring the morphology and microstructure of the samples. Fig. 2 (a) presented the TEM image of CDs together with the particle size distribution, the CDs appeared as spherical shapes without aggregation, with diameters in the region of 2–12 nm and the majority smaller than 10 nm. The hydrothermal time-dependent experiments (6 h as well as 12 h) were performed for 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 for the purpose of studying the morphological evolution of the catalyst samples. The 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 at 6 h of reaction was assembled from many nanosheets that were tightly packed together and organized into spherical microstructures (Fig. 2(b)). The ethylene glycol acted here as a solvent as well as a complexing agent to form a chain structure on the microcrystal surface by covalent or hydrogen bonding [31]. During the reaction, the Bi2WO6 nuclei were formed first and treated as seeds for the growth of Bi2WO6 nanosheets in supersaturated solution. After increasing the reaction time to 12 h, the nanosheets with high anisotropic surface energy were induced by ethylene glycol to start the Ostwald ripening process from solid spherical structure to hollow spherical structure from the inside out (Fig. 2(c)), thus the samples exhibit a three-dimensional nano-hollow microsphere shape [32]. Fig. S2 demonstrated the SEM images of Bi2W6 and Cl–Bi2WO6, respectively, in which both of them exhibit nanoflower hollow microspheres as well. In comparison with the irregular blocky and thick flaky stacked morphology before modification, Cl–Bi2WO6 exhibited different degrees of particle aggregation morphology. The TEM image of the composite 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 displayed a hollow spherical structure in Fig. 2(d), which agreed with the SEM pattern. The lattice stripe spacings d = 0.315 nm and d = 0.272 nm exhibited well-resolved in Fig. 2(e) and specialized to the (131) and (020) crystal planes of Bi2WO6, respectively. Furthermore, Fig. 2(f) exhibited a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm corresponded to the (002) plane of the CDs. Accordingly, SEM, TEM and HRTEM analyses verified that the CDs were successfully introduced into the catalyst surface.

The elemental components together with the chemical conditions of the Bi2WO6, Cl–Bi2WO6 and 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 catalysts were revealed by XPS analysis. The total XPS map identified composites consisting of Bi, W, O, C and Cl elements (Fig. S3a). In Fig. 3 (a), the fitted peaks at 159.0 eV and 164.4 eV were allocated to Bi 4f 7/2 and Bi 4f 5/2, respectively, which demonstrated the presence of Bi3+ in the crystal structure. The fitted peaks at 37.3 eV and 35.2 eV for W 4f 5/2 and W 4f 7/2 were correlated to W6+ (Fig. 3(b)). The Bi 4f as well as W 4f peaks in 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 presented a slight (0.18 ev) shift toward higher binding energies than the other two, which was likely attributed to the association between CDs and Cl–Bi2WO6 [33]. It was identified that the O1s spectrum of 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 was depolymerized into three peaks (Fig. 3(c)), of which 533.1 eV and 531.9 eV were classified as CO and O–H bonds. Additionally the broad peaks of the O1s spectra of all three composites were at around 530.1 eV, this corresponded to BiO bonds peaks from the [Bi2O2]2+ crystal structure. The peak located at 285.1 eV in Bi2WO6 as well as Cl–Bi2WO6 was attributed to hydrocarbons in the XPS instrument since shown in Fig. 3(d). In contrast, three groups of peaks 286.5 eV, 285.0 eV and 288.7 eV in the C 1s spectrum of 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 generated at C–O, C–C and CO, respectively, which validated the successful introduction of CDs in 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6. The Cl 2p spectrum of 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 was displayed in Fig. S3b, where the elemental presence of Cl was manifested by the binding energy peaks at ∼199.0 eV and ∼197.0 eV, and corresponds to Cl 2p 1/2 and Cl 2p 3/2, respectively [34]. The characterization described above verified that the composite catalyst does contain CDs and Cl. The 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 was furthermore composed of CDs and Cl–Bi2WO6 rather than other materials as seen from XRD, FT-IR, Raman, electron micrographs and XPS analysis.

UV–Vis DRS was conducted to observe the optical absorption features of each BWO. The specimens were noticed to absorb at wavelengths above 400 nm in Fig. 4 (a), which confirmed the visible light response of the collected products. The absorption edge of Cl–Bi2WO6 was red-shifted from 420 nm to 433 nm along the length direction by comparison with Bi2WO6. Furthermore, the absorption edges at 450 nm, 462 nm, 470 nm and 500 nm correspond to 0.1%, 0.3%, 0.5% and 1% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, respectively. CDs served as photosensitizers and centers for reflecting visible light, thus BWO fitted with CDs and Cl elements enabled efficient degradation by facilitating the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs with improved utilization of visible light. The color of primordial Cl–Bi2WO6 switched from white to brownish-yellow as the CDs were introduced, which was corresponded to the alteration of light absorption (illustrations displayed in Fig. 4(a)). A graph of (αhυ)2 versus photon energy (hυ) (Fig. 4(b)) indicated that Bi2WO6, Cl–Bi2WO6 and 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 with band gap Eg of ∼2.98 eV, ∼2.93 eV and ∼2.86 eV, respectively. Electrochemical Mott-Schottky (MS) measurements (Fig. S4) were undertaken to explore the type of semiconductor as well as the flat-band (EF) potential. Evidently the slope of the straight line part of the curve was positive indicating that Bi2WO6 was an n-type semiconductor. The EF potentials of the three polymers with reference to Ag/AgCl were −0.55 V, −0.53 V and −0.51 V, respectively. The reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) potential can be derived using the following equation:
(1)
E
RHE = E
Ag/AgCl + 0.198 V + 0.0592 pH
where the pH of the Na2SO4 electrolyte was 6.8 [35–37]. Thereby, the Ef values of the three were 0.05eV, 0.07eV and 0.09eV, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photovoltaic and photo (electric) chemical property analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To further examined the reason for the strengthened photocatalytic capability of 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, photoelectrochemical measurements were conducted. The fluorescent luminescence of semiconductors was evoked by the reconnection of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Ordinarily, a greater intensity of the luminescence peak in the spectrum denoted a higher rate of compounding of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The PL spectrum of the catalysts were represented in Fig. 5 (a), where the fluorescence intensity of each BWO exhibited a decreasing trend followed by an increasing trend, with 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 was the lowest threshold value. Presence of Cl elements encouraged charge transfer from Cl–Bi2WO6 to CDs, and the conjugated π structure of CDs largely inhibited the complexation of photogenerated carriers [38].

Photostability as well as reproducibility of the compounds can be confirmed by the photocurrent transient response spectra in Fig. 5(b). The 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 had the highest photocurrent response value in comparison to the rest of the BWO. The incorporation of Cl and CDs contributed to the transfer and separation of electron-hole pairs in visible light, whereas CDs to enhance the light utilization of the composites. Electrical chemical impedance (EIS) tests were also presented to explore the electricity resistance of photocatalyst charge exchange. The circle radius of the 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 sample was smaller than the rest of Bi2WO6 (Fig. 5(c)), which indicated that it facilitated the migration of more photogenerated carriers. It was evidenced that interfacial charge transfer can be facilitated by the injection of Cl elements and CDs. Interestingly, the electrochemical characteristics of the sample 1% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 were somewhat poorer as compared to 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, which may be contributed to the reduction of active sites caused by the high content of CDs. Above presented effects pointed out that the separation of photogenerated carriers in the synthesis material was benefitted by the entry of Cl elements and CDs, which resulted in the improvement of photocatalytic intensity.

The photocatalytic degradation intensity of each BWO was determined by degrading TCH under visible light irradiation. Initially, the TCH suspension contained catalyst was configured under darkness and individual BWO would attain adsorption-desorption equilibrium after 30 min (Fig. S5). Additional removal of TCH by dark adsorption reactions reached 29.85%, 34.61%, 36.84%, 36.00%, 38.71% and 33.29%, respectively, which indicated that the π-π interaction of CDs promoted the synthesized material's adsorption of TCH. After 60 min of illumination, the photocatalytic degradation capacities of Bi2WO6, Cl–Bi2WO6, 0.1% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, 0.3% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 and 1% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 were 59.62%, 64.15%, 67.82%, 70.55%, 85.10%, and 75.46%, respectively, based on Fig. 6 (a). It was noticeably that the degradation of Cl–Bi2WO6 was better in comparison with Bi2WO6, which explained that the catalytic efficiency would be enlarged by using the KCI-modified Bi2WO6 photocatalyst. As the CDs were incorporated, CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 had a superior photocatalytic capacity than that of Cl–Bi2WO6, which meaned that the insertion of CDs could dramatically accelerate the separation of photogenerated carriers and boost the degradation efficiency. Nevertheless, as the CDs content changed from 0.02 g to 0.15 g, the degradation efficiency of the composite increased by 25.48% and then decreased by 9.64%, which surprisingly was consistent with the electrochemical test results. It was therefore speculated that this was determined by the decreased active site induced by the excessive loading of CDs. The photodegradation kinetics of TCH by photocatalysts was also observed. Transaction kinetics were quantified by the Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) equation：
(2)
k t = − ln
(
C t
C o
)
where k was the apparent reaction speed constant, t was the irradiation time, Ct was at time t the concentration of TCH, and C0 was the original concentration. This specific value listed in Table S1 revealed that the 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 composite had the highest photodegradation rate, which was 2.1, 1.9, 1.7, 1.6 and 1.4 times higher than that of Bi2WO6, Cl–Bi2WO6, 0.1% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, 0.3% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, and 1% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6, respectively. A comparison of 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 as the most optimal photocatalyst with others were eventually accomplished in Table 1 . Interestingly, the catalyst of the present work had a high-level of photocatalytic activity.

Scavenger-quenching experiments were conduced to identify the key reactive substances (RSs) yielded by 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 during the catalytic process to illuminate the photocatalytic mechanism of 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6. In contrast to the blank control cohort, EDTA-2Na grossly depressed the photocatalytic reactivity, BQ partially inhibited the photocatalytic reaction, while IPA together with KBrO3 had relatively slight impacts on the degradation procedure. The variations in the BQ and EDTA-2Na groups revealed that h+ and •O2‾ were the primary RSs during the photocatalytic oxidation (Fig. 6(b)) (see Scheme 1).

Potential photocatalytic mechanisms for the photodegradation of TCH by 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 were derived from the above experiments (Scheme 2
). When visible light irradiates the photocatalyst, the catalyst was stimulated to form electron-hole pairs. The conjugated π structure of CDs promoted the separation efficiency of charges and holes, which also acted as a photoelectron reservoir to depress the complexation of electron-hole pairs. In addition, photogenerated electrons were restrained around the chloride ions and later diverted by the CDs, which prevented the complexation with the holes to some extent. Subsequently, •O2‾ radicals were generated by electron binding to O2 trapped on the surface of CDs to decompose TCH. The reaction of •O2‾ with H2O may also generate further •OH, •O2‾ and •OH catalyze the oxidation of TCH. In parallel, TCH was dirigibly oxidized by the vacancies from Cl–Bi2WO6. Eventually, with the progression of the reaction TCH molecules were slowly disrupted and oxidized to small molecules of H2O and CO2, etc [44]. The following formula is shown:
O2 + e−→•O2‾
•O2‾ + H2O→•OH + OH− + O2
•OH + •O2‾ + h+ + TC→H2O + CO2

Stability of photocatalysts was probed. The stability of 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 was evaluated in four cycles of TCH depuration following visible light radiation. The degradation performance of the photocatalyst only deteriorated from 85.10% to 81.32% after four repeated cycles, which revealed that the formulated 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 composite photocatalyst was very reliable in the degradation procedure, which can be seen in Fig. 7 (a). Moreover, XRD analysis was conducted to augment the physical structure of the newly prepared and recuperated composites (Fig. 7(b)). No differences in the peaks between the freshly samples and the recovered samples were noticeable in the figure, thus revealing that 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 exhibited high photocatalytic stability and was capable of removing TCH over a longer reaction period.

BWO catalyst was satisfactorily constructed by hydrothermal method and was capable of degrading TCH in visible light. Electron microscopy patterns demonstrated that the compound were in the form of 3D nano-hollow microspheres and CDs were successfully anchored on the surface of Cl–Bi2WO6. The strengthened photocatalytic degradation of TCH (up to 85.1% removal) was accomplished by 0.5% CDs/Cl–Bi2WO6 with more reactant sites in comparison to Bi2WO6 and Cl–Bi2WO6, while the reinforced charge separation was verified by EIS and PL spectroscopy. Even after four cycles, the catalyst still possessed outstanding degradation performance and the main RSs existed in the complex system were •O2‾, •OH and h+. Eventually, a plausible photocatalytic mechanism for the photodegradation of TCH was proffered. This design proposaled a new strategy for the exploitation of bismuth-based composite catalysts for TCH remediation in ecological environments.

Fanyong Yan and Yao Wang conducted the conception and conceived the experiments. Yao Wang, Chunhui Yi, Jinxia Xu, Bowei Wang, Ran Ma, and Ming Xu performed or cooperated with the experiments, discussed and solved the difficulties encountered during the experiments. Fanyong Yan, Yao Wang and Chunhui Yi handled the data, while Yao Wang accomplished the writing of the manuscript. Fanyong Yan, Chunhui Yi, Ran Ma, Jinxia Xu, Bowei Wang and Ming Xu offered revisions to the manuscript.
The data presented in this study are available within this article. Further inquiries may be directed to the authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678409), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology of China (19JCYBJC19800, and 15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5042).
